title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Ancient Art of the Hunt: Falconry's Living Tradition
content.text: Every autumn, in the valleys of rural Kazakhstan, men on horseback ride out at dawn with golden eagles perched on their gloved fists. The birds are not pets; they are trained hunting partners, capable of striking prey as large as a fox or small deer. This is falconry — one of the oldest documented relationships between humans and birds of prey. (37) [37] La cetrería es reconocida como patrimonio cultural inmaterial. [/37] In 2016, UNESCO added falconry to its list of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, acknowledging its living significance in communities across more than a dozen countries. || The origins of falconry are disputed, but most historians place its emergence in Central Asia or the Middle East around three thousand years ago. From there it spread westward along trade routes, reaching Persia, Arabia and eventually medieval Europe. (38) [38] En Europa medieval, la cetrería era símbolo de estatus social. [/38] At the courts of English and French kings, the species of bird a person could fly was strictly governed by rank: a king might fly an eagle, a knight a falcon, and a servant only a kestrel. || Training a bird of prey requires extraordinary patience and skill. A wild hawk or falcon must first learn to trust its trainer completely — a process called 'manning' that can take weeks of slow, careful handling. (39) [39] El proceso de adiestramiento exige dedicación diaria durante meses. [/39] The bird must learn to return reliably to the falconer's fist and, eventually, to pursue and return with quarry. Even experienced falconers sometimes spend years perfecting their technique with a new bird. || Modern falconry has adapted to a world in which hunting for food is no longer a necessity. Many falconers today practise the sport purely for its cultural and traditional value, and competitions judged on the style and performance of the birds attract participants from across the globe. (40) [40] La cetrería competitiva ha cobrado protagonismo internacional. [/40] In the Gulf states, falcon beauty contests and racing events draw enormous crowds and significant prize money. || Conservation has become an increasingly important dimension of the falconry community. Several raptor species were seriously depleted during the twentieth century, and falconers have been among the most committed advocates for their protection. (41) [41] Los cetreros contribuyen activamente a la conservación de rapaces. [/41] Captive breeding programmes run by licensed falconers have helped restore populations of peregrine falcons and other threatened species in several countries. || The bond between falconer and bird remains at the heart of the tradition. (42) [42] La relación entre humano y ave trasciende la caza. [/42] 'The bird can leave at any moment,' says one Mongolian falconer. 'The fact that it chooses to return is what makes this extraordinary.'

paragraphs.A: Regular reintroduction projects backed by falconers' associations have measurably improved raptor numbers in the wild.
paragraphs.B: It is less a sport than a relationship — one built on mutual respect rather than dominance.
paragraphs.C: It was considered such a noble pursuit that it featured prominently in illuminated manuscripts and royal portraits.
paragraphs.D: Feeding schedules, exercise routines and careful weight management must all be maintained consistently throughout the season.
paragraphs.E: International falconry festivals now take place on several continents, drawing practitioners from remarkably diverse cultural backgrounds.
paragraphs.F: The sport is also practised as a form of pest control, with trained raptors deployed at airports and farms to deter smaller birds.
paragraphs.G: Its recognition as a shared human heritage reflects its survival across millennia and across vastly different cultures.

questions.explanation: G connects the antiquity of falconry with its recognition by UNESCO as shared intangible heritage of humanity.
C describes the noble status of falconry in medieval Europe, supported by manuscripts and royal portraits.
D specifies the daily care (feeding, exercise, weight) required during the bird's month-long training.
E introduces the international falconry festivals that reflect the global competitive prominence mentioned before.
A describes the reintroduction projects backed by falconers that have improved raptor populations.
B captures the nature of the relationship as mutual respect, preparing the final quote from the Mongolian falconer.
